seo.title: Legal Code Generation AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for code generation AI agents in legal. Covers Attorney-client privilege protections, Bar association ethics rules, Court filing standards.

content.intro: Deploying AI agents for legal code generation sits at the intersection of two high-stakes domains: the ethical and malpractice obligations of legal practice, and the security and correctness requirements of production software. A single hallucinated case citation or a credential leak in generated code can trigger bar sanctions, malpractice liability, or client data breaches simultaneously. This checklist provides concrete, enforceable controls to ensure your AI agent meets both legal professional responsibility standards and software engineering safety requirements.

sections.heading: Attorney Oversight & Legal Compliance Controls
sections.description: Ensure every AI-generated legal artifact passes through licensed attorney review and meets professional responsibility obligations before reaching clients or courts.

items.label: Mandatory Licensed Attorney Review Gate
items.description: Implement a hard workflow gate that requires a licensed attorney to explicitly approve any AI-generated legal document, brief, or code-based legal tool before it is sent to a client, filed with a court, or used in a matter. This gate must be logged with attorney bar number, timestamp, and matter ID.

items.label: Citation Verification Against Live Legal Databases
items.description: Before any generated content containing case citations is surfaced to a reviewer, automatically query Westlaw, LexisNexis, or CourtListener APIs to confirm each cited case exists, has not been overruled, and the quoted holding matches the actual opinion. Reject output and re-prompt if any citation fails verification.

items.label: Hallucination Risk Disclosure in UI
items.description: Display a persistent, non-dismissible banner in the attorney review interface stating that AI-generated legal content may contain fabricated citations or misstatements of law, referencing the Mata v. Avianca precedent as a documented failure mode. Attorneys must acknowledge this warning before approving output.

items.label: Bar Association Ethics Rule Mapping
items.description: Maintain a jurisdiction-specific mapping of applicable Model Rules of Professional Conduct (e.g., ABA Model Rules 1.1 Competence, 1.6 Confidentiality, 5.5 Unauthorized Practice) and validate that the agent's use case does not violate any rule in the attorney's licensed jurisdiction before deployment.

items.label: Court Filing Format Compliance Check
items.description: For any code or document intended for court submission, run an automated check against the target court's local rules (font, margin, page limits, caption format, certificate of service requirements) using a rules database updated at least monthly.

items.label: Unauthorized Practice of Law (UPL) Boundary Enforcement
items.description: Configure the agent to refuse to generate jurisdiction-specific legal advice, court strategy recommendations, or client-facing legal opinions without an attorney in the loop. Define and enforce a strict prompt classification layer that routes UPL-risk prompts to attorney escalation rather than direct generation.

items.label: eDiscovery Protocol Compliance for Generated Code
items.description: When the agent generates code used in eDiscovery workflows (e.g., document review scripts, data extraction tools), validate that the code preserves metadata, maintains chain of custody logging, and does not alter document hash values, in compliance with FRCP Rule 26 and applicable ESI protocols.

sections.heading: Client Confidentiality & Data Governance
sections.description: Protect attorney-client privilege and prevent client confidential information from being exposed to model training pipelines, third-party APIs, or unauthorized personnel.

items.label: Training Data Opt-Out Enforcement
items.description: Contractually and technically enforce that no client matter data, prompts containing client facts, or generated outputs tied to a specific matter are used for LLM fine-tuning or RLHF feedback loops. Implement data tagging at ingestion to flag matter-specific content and exclude it from any training pipeline.

items.label: Attorney-Client Privilege Metadata Tagging
items.description: Automatically tag all inputs and outputs processed by the agent with privilege classification (attorney-client privileged, work product, non-privileged) based on matter context. Ensure privileged content is encrypted at rest and in transit and is excluded from any logging system accessible to non-attorney personnel.

items.label: Third-Party LLM API Data Residency Validation
items.description: Before routing any prompt containing client information to an external LLM API (e.g., OpenAI, Anthropic, Google), verify that the API provider's data processing agreement prohibits training on submitted data, specifies data residency in a compliant jurisdiction, and has executed a BAA or equivalent legal agreement.

items.label: PII and Confidential Data Scrubbing in Prompts
items.description: Deploy a pre-processing layer that detects and redacts or pseudonymizes client names, case numbers, Social Security numbers, financial account details, and other PII from prompts before they are sent to the LLM. Log all redaction events for audit purposes.

items.label: Matter-Scoped Access Controls
items.description: Enforce role-based access controls so that the agent can only access documents, context, and prior outputs scoped to the specific matter the attorney is working on. Cross-matter data bleed must be technically impossible, not just policy-prohibited.

items.label: Audit Log Retention for Privilege Review
items.description: Retain immutable audit logs of all agent interactions, including prompts, outputs, attorney review decisions, and citation verification results, for a minimum of 7 years or the jurisdiction's applicable record retention period, whichever is longer. Logs must be producible in eDiscovery.

items.label: Conflict of Interest Screening Integration
items.description: Before the agent processes any new matter context, trigger an automated conflict check against the firm's conflict database to ensure the AI system does not inadvertently surface confidential information from a conflicted client's matter into the current engagement.

sections.heading: Code Generation Safety & Sandbox Execution
sections.description: Ensure all AI-generated code is syntactically valid, free of security vulnerabilities, and executed only within isolated environments that cannot affect production systems or expose sensitive data.

items.label: Isolated Sandbox Execution with No Network Egress
items.description: Execute all AI-generated code in a containerized sandbox (e.g., gVisor, Firecracker microVM, or equivalent) with network egress completely disabled. The sandbox must have no access to production databases, client file systems, or external APIs during test execution.

items.label: Credential and Secret Leakage Scanning
items.description: Before executing or returning any generated code, run a static scan using tools such as Trufflehog, Gitleaks, or Semgrep secret detection rules to identify hardcoded API keys, passwords, tokens, or connection strings. Reject and re-generate any code containing detected secrets.

items.label: Dependency Vulnerability Assessment
items.description: For every library or package imported in generated code, query the OSV (Open Source Vulnerabilities) database and the NIST NVD to check for known CVEs with CVSS score 7.0 or higher. Block execution and flag for attorney-developer review if any high or critical vulnerability is detected in a dependency.

items.label: Syntax Validation Before Execution
items.description: Run language-specific static analysis (e.g., pylint/mypy for Python, ESLint for JavaScript, javac dry-run for Java) on all generated code before sandbox execution. Surface syntax errors back to the agent for iterative refinement rather than allowing malformed code to reach the execution layer.

items.label: Resource Limit Enforcement in Sandbox
items.description: Enforce hard limits on CPU time (max 30 seconds), memory (max 512MB), and disk I/O within the sandbox to prevent denial-of-service conditions from runaway generated code. Log all resource limit violations and alert the supervising engineer.

items.label: Automated Test Coverage Requirement
items.description: Require the agent to generate unit tests alongside functional code, and enforce a minimum test pass rate of 100% for generated tests before code is presented to the attorney reviewer. Tests must cover at least the primary function, one edge case, and one error condition.

items.label: Code Output Provenance Logging
items.description: Log the full generation chain for every code artifact: the original prompt, model version, temperature settings, number of refinement iterations, test results, and final output hash. This provenance record must be attached to the matter file to support future audit or malpractice defense.

items.label: Iterative Refinement Loop Termination Control
items.description: Set a maximum of 5 refinement iterations for the agent's self-correction loop. If the code does not pass all validation checks within 5 iterations, escalate to a human engineer rather than continuing to iterate, to prevent infinite loops and ensure human oversight of persistent failures.

sections.heading: Incident Response & Ongoing Governance
sections.description: Establish clear procedures for detecting, responding to, and learning from failures specific to legal AI code generation, including malpractice triggers, citation errors, and security incidents.

items.label: Hallucinated Citation Incident Response Playbook
items.description: Maintain a documented playbook for responding to a discovered hallucinated citation, including: immediate withdrawal of the affected filing if possible, client notification within 24 hours, bar association self-reporting assessment with outside ethics counsel, and a root cause analysis of why citation verification failed.

items.label: Malpractice Insurance Coverage Verification for AI Use
items.description: Confirm with the firm's malpractice insurer in writing that AI-assisted legal work is covered under the current policy. Obtain a coverage opinion specifically addressing AI-generated code used in legal workflows. Review coverage annually as AI use expands.

items.label: Model Version Change Review Process
items.description: Establish a formal review process triggered whenever the underlying LLM is updated or replaced. The review must include re-running the full citation verification test suite, re-validating code generation safety controls, and obtaining attorney sign-off before the new model version is used in live matters.

items.label: Quarterly Bias and Accuracy Audits
items.description: Conduct quarterly audits of a random sample of agent outputs (minimum 50 outputs per audit) to assess citation accuracy, legal reasoning quality, and code correctness. Engage an independent licensed attorney not involved in day-to-day use to review the sample and document findings.

items.label: Client Disclosure and Informed Consent Protocol
items.description: Develop a standardized client disclosure document explaining that AI tools are used in the engagement, what data is processed, and what human oversight is applied. Obtain signed informed consent before using the AI agent on any client matter, and retain consent records in the matter file.

items.label: Security Incident Notification for Code Execution Breaches
items.description: Define a security incident response procedure specifically for sandbox escape or credential exposure events originating from generated code. The procedure must include containment within 1 hour, client notification within 72 hours per applicable breach notification laws, and forensic preservation of the generated code artifact.

items.label: Annual Attorney Competency Training on AI Tools
items.description: Require all attorneys using the AI agent to complete annual training covering: how the model generates output, known failure modes (including citation hallucination), their professional responsibility obligations when supervising AI, and the firm's specific review and approval procedures. Document completion in personnel records.

regulatoryNotes.heading: Regulatory Considerations
notes: ABA Model Rule 1.1 (Competence) has been interpreted by multiple state bars to require attorneys to understand the benefits and risks of relevant technology, including AI tools. Attorneys supervising AI-generated legal code must be able to evaluate its correctness and cannot delegate that judgment entirely to the system. Failure to exercise competent supervision is a disciplinable offense regardless of whether the AI or the attorney made the underlying error.

The Mata v. Avianca (S.D.N.Y. 2023) decision established that attorneys are personally responsible for citations submitted to courts, even when those citations were generated by AI. Courts have imposed sanctions under FRCP Rule 11 for filing briefs containing non-existent cases. Any deployment of legal AI must include citation verification as a non-negotiable pre-submission control, and attorneys must personally verify each citation rather than relying solely on automated checks.

Client confidential information is protected under ABA Model Rule 1.6 and equivalent state rules, which prohibit disclosure without informed consent. Routing client data through third-party LLM APIs may constitute a disclosure under some state bar interpretations unless the provider has contractually agreed to confidentiality protections equivalent to those owed to the client. Firms must obtain ethics opinions from their state bar or outside ethics counsel before using cloud-based LLMs on client matters.

Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 26(g) requires that eDiscovery certifications be made by an attorney who has conducted a reasonable inquiry. AI-generated code used in document review or data processing workflows does not satisfy this inquiry requirement on its own. The supervising attorney must understand what the code does, validate its output against a sample, and be prepared to defend the methodology in court. Code provenance logs and test results are essential to supporting this defense.

cta.heading: Automate Your Legal Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your legal AI agent against this checklist, automatically flag citation verification failures, sandbox breaches, and missing attorney review gates before they become malpractice exposure.
